prediction.reasoning: Miami receives ~62 inches annually on average, with significant seasonal variation. Wet season (May-October) brings tropical systems and afternoon thunderstorms; dry season (November-April) is much drier. P10 represents drier years, P90 represents wetter years with active hurricane seasons or enhanced tropical activity.

Miami-Dade County (Homestead) experiences subtropical climate with wet summers (June-September) and dry winters. Climatological annual mean ~60 inches. High interannual variability due to tropical systems, El Niño/La Niña effects, and occasional hurricanes. P10-P90 range reflects this natural variability and epistemic uncertainty about specific station microclimate.

Gulf coast Florida cities receive 50-60 inches annually on average. Summer monsoon season (June-September) dominates with 25-30 inches. Winter/spring contributes 15-20 inches. Tropical systems add variability. P10 reflects drier years, P90 reflects wetter years with active hurricane season.

North Port is on Florida's Gulf coast (Charlotte County). Annual rainfall averages 50-55 inches, with summer monsoon (June-September) contributing ~40% and scattered winter/spring precipitation. P10 accounts for dry years; P90 for wet years with active tropical systems. Uncertainty reflects natural variability and local microclimatic factors.

Florida averages 50-60 inches annually. Southern Florida (Miami area) receives ~60 inches; northern Florida (Jacksonville) ~52 inches. Without knowing the specific city, using statewide average. P10 represents drier years, P90 represents wetter years with tropical systems. Confidence moderate due to location uncertainty and natural variability.

Florida's annual rainfall averages 50-60 inches statewide, with spatial and interannual variability. FAWN stations span diverse climates: wetter south/central (60+ in), drier northwest (45-50 in). Uncertainty reflects unknown station location, ENSO/Atlantic hurricane activity, and natural year-to-year variance. P10-P90 range captures ~80% of expected outcomes.

Miami's June-September period coincides with the Atlantic hurricane season and summer monsoon patterns. Average monthly rainfall is 6-9 inches during this window. June typically sees 9-10 inches, July-August 8-9 inches each, and September 7-8 inches. Total median ~38 inches. Dry years (p10) ~28 inches; wet years with tropical systems (p90) ~50 inches.

Miami-Dade County (Homestead) experiences peak summer monsoon rainfall June-September. Climatologically, this 4-month window receives ~27-30 inches. High variability due to tropical systems (hurricanes, tropical storms) and sea-breeze convection. P10 reflects dry years; P90 reflects active hurricane seasons. Confidence moderate due to extreme event uncertainty.

Gulf coast Florida (June-Sept) is the peak wet season with frequent afternoon thunderstorms and tropical systems. Typical summer rainfall is 40-55 inches; p10 accounts for drier years, p90 for active hurricane seasons or persistent wet patterns. Mid-sized coastal city experiences similar patterns to larger metros.

North Port (Charlotte County) on Florida's Gulf coast receives peak rainfall June-September during Atlantic hurricane season and summer convection. Climatological mean ~27-29 inches for this 4-month window. High interannual variability due to hurricane activity. P10/P90 span reflects both dry years and wet years with tropical systems.

Florida's June-September period is the wet season, driven by tropical moisture, afternoon thunderstorms, and occasional hurricanes. Statewide average is ~38-40 inches. Coastal areas and southern Florida tend wetter; inland areas slightly drier. Without specific location, using statewide typical range.

Florida's June-September period captures peak summer monsoon and hurricane season. FAWN stations span diverse climates (wet Panhandle to drier south). Typical summer rainfall averages 25-30 inches statewide. High uncertainty due to unknown station location and interannual variability from tropical systems.

Miami's winter (Dec-Feb) is the dry season with minimal tropical activity. December averages ~2.5", January ~2.0", February ~2.5" monthly, totaling ~7" climatologically. However, variability exists from occasional cold fronts and rare winter storms. P10 reflects drier-than-normal conditions, P50 the typical expectation, P90 accounts for wetter years with frontal systems.

Miami-Dade winter (Dec-Feb) is dry season with occasional frontal systems. Climatological mean ~4-6 inches. High variability from tropical systems and cold fronts. P10 reflects dry years, P90 accounts for active frontal activity and rare tropical influence.

Gulf coast Florida (100k city, likely Pensacola/Tallahassee area) receives winter rainfall from frontal systems and occasional tropical systems. Dec-Feb is moderate rainfall season. Typical winter totals: 6-14 inches. P50 ~9.5" reflects average winter precipitation. P10 ~6.5" for drier winters, P90 ~13.5" for wetter systems.

North Port (Charlotte County) on Florida's Gulf coast receives winter rainfall from frontal systems and occasional tropical systems. Dec-Feb climatology averages ~9-10 inches. P10 reflects drier winters; P90 accounts for active frontal patterns and rare tropical influence. Moderate confidence due to high interannual variability in winter precipitation.

Florida's winter (Dec-Feb) is the dry season. Most of the state receives 6-14 inches during this 3-month period. The state's subtropical location means winter rainfall is moderate, driven by occasional cold fronts and weak systems. Southern Florida tends slightly drier; northern areas slightly wetter. Without knowing the specific city, using statewide averages: p10 represents dry winters, p50 the

Florida's Dec-Feb rainfall varies by location. Northern FL averages 5-7 inches; southern FL 4-6 inches. Winter is relatively dry compared to summer. FAWN stations span diverse climates. P10-P90 range reflects regional variability and interannual uncertainty. Confidence moderate due to unknown specific station location within the 50-station network.

Miami averages ~62 inches/year. It has a strong wet season (May–Oct) driven by convective storms and hurricanes. Dry years (~45in) occur during La Niña or weak Atlantic hurricane seasons; wet years (~80in) during active hurricane seasons or strong El Niño. Historical data supports this range.

Homestead/Miami-Dade sits in South Florida's wet-dry tropical climate. Mean annual rainfall ~60-65". Dry years (La Niña, weak hurricane seasons) can drop to ~45". Wet years with active Atlantic hurricanes/tropical storms push toward 80"+. FAWN stn 440 is inland agricultural area, slightly drier than coastal Miami.

Gulf coast FL city ~100k (likely Fort Myers, Cape Coral, or Sarasota area). These cities average ~53-57"/yr. Wet season Jun-Sep dominates. P50~55", dry year ~42", wet year (active hurricane/storm season) ~70". Uncertainty from city ambiguity and interannual variability.

North Port, FL (Sarasota Co., Gulf coast) averages ~55in/yr. Wet season (Jun-Sep) dominates. Dry years (~42in) occur in La Niña/drought; wet years (~70in) with active hurricane seasons or El Niño. Uncertainty from station-specific microclimates and interannual variability (ENSO, tropical storms).

Florida statewide avg ~54in/yr. North FL ~52in, South FL ~60in, Central ~53in. Unknown location adds uncertainty. P10 reflects dry year/north FL; P50 is statewide median; P90 reflects wet year or south FL. Confidence reduced due to unknown city.

Florida statewide annual rainfall ~54in median. FAWN stations span dry Panhandle (~50in) to wet South FL (~60in). Epistemic uncertainty from random station draw widens spread. Aleatoric variability (~±10in/yr) adds further spread. P10 reflects dry year in drier region; P90 reflects wet year in wetter region.

Miami's wet season (Jun–Sep) averages ~7–8 in/month, totaling ~30 in over 4 months. P10 (~22 in) reflects dry years with suppressed convection; P90 (~40 in) reflects active hurricane/tropical seasons. Miami averages ~62 in/year, with ~60% falling Jun–Sep.

Homestead/Miami-Dade is in South Florida's wet season (Jun–Sep), typically receiving ~28–36" over these 4 months. P50~32" reflects median climatology. P10~22" accounts for dry anomalies/drought years; P90~44" reflects active hurricane/tropical seasons. High interannual variability driven by ENSO and Atlantic hurricane activity.

Gulf coast FL cities ~100k (e.g. Fort Myers, Cape Coral, Sarasota, Naples) get heavy summer rain Jun–Sep. Monthly avg ~7–9in; 4-month total ~30–34in median. Dry years ~22in, wet/hurricane years ~44in. Gulf coast slightly wetter than Atlantic side in summer due to sea breezes.

North Port is on SW Florida's Gulf coast, one of FL's wettest regions. Jun–Sep is peak wet season driven by sea breezes, afternoon convection, and tropical systems. Median ~32" reflects ~8"/month avg. P10 ~22" for dry years; P90 ~44" accounts for active hurricane/tropical seasons. Spread reflects interannual variability and limited station-specific data.

Florida's Jun–Sep rainy season averages ~30–35" statewide. South FL (Miami) gets ~25–30", Central (Orlando) ~30–35", North (Jacksonville) ~25–30". Unknown location adds uncertainty. Median ~32" is a reasonable statewide estimate. Dry years ~22", wet/hurricane years ~45"+.

FL wet season (Jun-Sep) dominates annual rainfall. South FL averages ~35-40", north ~25-30". A random FAWN station adds cross-state epistemic spread. P50~34" reflects statewide average; P10~22" covers dry years/north stations; P90~50" covers wet years/south stations or active hurricane seasons.

Miami's dry season runs Dec–Feb. Monthly normals ~2–2.5" each, totaling ~6–7" median over 3 months. Dry years can see ~4–5" total; wet/El Niño winters can bring 12–15"+. Dec–Feb is influenced by cold fronts, which can spike rainfall in wet years.

Homestead/Miami-Dade Dec-Feb is Florida's dry season. Median ~5-6" over 3 months. Dry years (La Niña, weak fronts) yield ~2". Wet years (El Niño-enhanced frontal activity) can push 10-12". High interannual variability driven by ENSO and cold front frequency.

Gulf coast FL cities ~100k (e.g. Fort Myers, Cape Coral, Sarasota, Naples) get ~2–3"/mo Dec–Feb. Median ~8.5" total. Dry winters common (p10~4.5"), but cold fronts/El Niño can bring heavy rain (p90~15"). Uncertainty from unknown exact city and interannual variability.

North Port, SW Florida Gulf coast. Dec-Feb is dry season; monthly avg ~2in each. Median ~5.5in total. Dry years (cold fronts scarce) yield ~2.5in; wet years with active frontal passages or rare tropical influence push to ~10.5in. High interannual variability driven by ENSO and frontal frequency.

Florida's Dec–Feb is its dry season. North FL (~Tallahassee) averages ~12–14" for 3 months; South FL (~Miami) ~5–6". Statewide median ~9" accounts for this spread. P10 ~4.5" (dry south/dry year), P90 ~16" (north FL or wet La Niña winter). Confidence reduced due to unknown location within FL.

FL Dec-Feb is the dry season; statewide avg ~3-4"/month but varies N-S. North FL (Panhandle) gets more winter rain (~5-6"/season); South FL gets less (~3-4"). Random FAWN station adds location uncertainty. Dry years can see <4", wet years (strong fronts/La Niña) can exceed 12-15". Median ~7.5" reflects statewide blend.

Miami's climate normal annual rainfall is about 61 inches, dominated by the May-October wet season. Year-to-year variability is substantial due to tropical systems and ENSO, so a dry year may fall near 48 in and a wet year near 75 in.

Homestead, Miami-Dade has a subtropical wet climate averaging ~55-60 in/yr, dominated by May-Oct wet season convection and tropical systems. High interannual variability from hurricanes/ENSO widens tails. Median ~56 in; dry years ~42, wet/storm years ~72.

A Gulf coast Florida city of ~100k (e.g., Cape Coral, Port St. Lucie-adjacent, Pensacola, Sarasota area) typically sees ~50-55 in/yr, dominated by summer wet-season convection and tropical systems. Interannual variability driven by ENSO and hurricane activity gives a roughly 44-68 in range for P10-P90.

North Port lies in southwest Gulf coast Florida with a humid subtropical/tropical climate. Annual averages ~52-55 in, dominated by a wet summer season (Jun-Sep) plus tropical systems. Year-to-year variability driven by ENSO and hurricane activity gives a wide spread; dry La Nina years near 42 in, wet/active hurricane years near 68 in.

Florida is humid subtropical with a wet summer season. Statewide annual rainfall averages roughly 50-55 inches, with most locations between ~45 and 65 inches. Without a known city, I use a statewide distribution centered near 54 inches, widening percentiles to capture spatial and year-to-year variability (e.g., wetter Gulf Coast vs drier Keys).

FAWN stations span Florida, where annual rainfall averages ~50-55 in. Variability comes from station location (panhandle/south wetter, central drier) and year-to-year swings driven by tropical systems and ENSO. Combined epistemic+aleatoric spread gives roughly 42-66 in for the 10th-90th range around a ~53 in median.

Miami's wet season (Jun-Sep) delivers most of its ~62 in/yr rainfall, driven by sea-breeze thunderstorms and tropical systems. Monthly totals average ~8-10 in, summing to ~36-40 in for the four months. Variability is high due to tropical storm/hurricane influence, widening the p90.

Homestead/South Miami-Dade has a strong wet season (Jun-Sep) driven by convection, sea breeze, and tropical systems. JJAS typically delivers ~60-65% of ~55-60 in annual rainfall, so ~30 in median. Wide spread reflects tropical cyclone influence and interannual variability.

A Gulf coast FL city ~100k (e.g., Cape Coral, Fort Myers area) gets heavy summer wet-season rain from sea breeze convection and occasional tropical systems. June-Sept is the bulk of annual rainfall, typically ~28-32 in median, with dry years near 22 and wet/storm years near 40+.

North Port sits on SW Gulf coast Florida, dominated by summer wet season. June-Sept is peak rainy season delivering ~60% of annual ~52in, driven by sea-breeze convection and tropical systems. Expect ~28-32in median, with high variability from tropical activity producing the wide upper tail.

June–Sept is Florida's wet season, contributing ~60% of annual rain via daily convection and tropical systems. Statewide monthly averages run ~6–8 in, so the 4-month total typically centers near 28 in. Spread reflects unknown city (panhandle vs peninsula vs Keys) and year-to-year/tropical variability.

June-Sept is Florida's wet season, delivering ~55-60% of annual rainfall (~50-55 in/yr). Typical JJAS totals run 25-30 in driven by daily convection, sea breeze, and tropical systems. Variability between stations and years (hurricanes, drought) widens the spread, hence p10~18, p90~40.

Miami's dry season runs Dec–Feb. Monthly normals are roughly 2 inches each (Dec ~2.2, Jan ~1.6, Feb ~2.3), totaling ~6 inches. High interannual variability driven by ENSO: El Niño winters wetter, La Niña drier. P10 reflects dry La Niña years, P90 wet El Niño years with passing fronts.

Homestead (Miami-Dade) DJF is the dry season; monthly rainfall ~1.5-2.5 in, totaling ~6-8 in median. ENSO drives strong variability: El Niño winters can exceed 12 in, La Niña winters can fall below 4 in. Right-skewed distribution reflects this.

A Gulf coast city of ~100k (likely Cape Coral, Port Charlotte, or Sarasota area) has a dry-season winter. DJF is the driest stretch in SW Florida, averaging ~2.5-3.5 in/month, totaling roughly 8-10 in. ENSO drives variability: El Niño winters can double rainfall (wet tail ~15 in), La Niña winters run dry (~5-6 in). Median near 9-10 in.

North Port (SW Gulf coast) has a dry winter season (DJF). Typical winter totals ~6-9 in, driven by frontal passages. High ENSO sensitivity: El Niño winters can double rainfall (12-16 in), La Niña winters can be very dry (3-4 in), widening the upper tail.

Florida winter (DJF) is the dry season. Statewide DJF totals typically run 7-10 inches, with north Florida wetter from frontal passages and south/peninsula drier, modulated by ENSO (El Niño wetter, La Niña drier). Unknown city widens the spread, so I center near ~8.5 in with a broad range.

DJF is Florida's dry season; rainfall driven by frontal passages. Statewide DJF totals typically 6-10 in, higher in north/panhandle and during El Niño winters, lower in south Florida. Large year-to-year and station variability widens the distribution.

Miami averages ~60-62 in/year, dominated by the May-Oct wet season with convective storms and tropical systems. Interannual variability is sizable (ENSO, hurricane activity): dry years fall to ~45-50 in, wet years with tropical landfalls can exceed 75-80 in. Quantiles reflect this right-skewed distribution.

Homestead/south Miami-Dade averages ~55-60 in/yr, with a wet season (May-Oct) supplying ~75% of totals. Interannual variability is large (tropical systems, ENSO), with dry years near 40-45 in and wet years exceeding 70-75 in. Quantiles reflect ~12 in spread around a ~57 in median.

Gulf coast FL cities (e.g., Cape Coral, Clearwater, Sarasota) average ~50-56 in/yr, dominated by June-Sept convective wet season plus tropical systems. Interannual variability (ENSO, hurricanes) gives dry years ~40-45 in and wet years ~65-70 in. Median ~54 in.

North Port (SW Florida Gulf coast, Sarasota County) averages ~52-55 in/yr, dominated by June-Sept convective wet season plus occasional tropical systems. Interannual variability (ENSO, hurricane landfalls) spans roughly 40-68 in. Median ~53 in; dry years ~42; wet years with tropical impacts ~66+.

Florida annual rainfall averages ~50-60 in: peninsula ~52-55, Panhandle ~60-65, Keys ~40. Unknown city widens spread. Combining station climatology variability (dry years ~40-45, wet years ~65-75, e.g., wet hurricane seasons) gives p10 ~42, median ~54, p90 ~68.

Florida annual rainfall averages ~50-55 in statewide; panhandle stations run 60-65, central peninsula 48-52, dry years can dip to ~40. Combining station-to-station spatial variation (FAWN spans the state) with interannual variability (ENSO, tropical cyclones) yields a fairly wide distribution centered near 53 inches.

Miami's wet season: June ~9-10", July ~6-7", Aug ~8-9", Sep ~9-10" climatological normals, summing to ~33-35". Interannual variability driven by tropical systems and convective activity gives a wide spread: dry years ~25", wet years (tropical storms/hurricanes) can exceed 45".

Homestead's wet season (Jun–Sep) averages ~7-9"/month from sea-breeze convection and tropical systems (Jun ~9", Jul ~7", Aug ~8", Sep ~9"), giving a median near 31". Interannual variability is large: dry years ~20-24", wet years with tropical cyclones can exceed 40".

A Gulf coast FL city of ~100k (e.g., Cape Coral/Fort Myers or Clearwater) gets ~8-9 in/month in the June-Sep wet season from daily sea-breeze thunderstorms, averaging ~32-35 in total. Interannual variability is large due to tropical systems: dry years ~25 in, wet years (landfalling storms) can exceed 44 in.

North Port lies in SW Florida's wet season core; June–Sept monthly normals run ~8-9 in from sea-breeze convection, totaling ~33 in. Interannual variability is high: dry summers yield ~22-25 in, while tropical storms/hurricanes can push totals to 45+ in. Quantiles reflect climatology plus tropical-system tail risk.

June–Sep is Florida's wet season, averaging ~7-8 in/month from daily convective storms plus tropical systems, giving typical totals of 26-32 in statewide. Unknown location widens uncertainty (drier inland north vs wetter southeast/panhandle), and hurricane landfalls can push totals well above 40 in, while a dry year may yield ~20 in.

June–Sep is Florida's wet season, averaging ~7 in/month from convective storms plus tropical systems, giving ~26–30 in typical totals. Station-to-station spread (panhandle/SE coast wetter, central drier) plus interannual variability (dry years ~20 in, hurricane-active years 38+ in) widens the distribution.

Dec–Feb is Miami's dry season, with monthly normals of roughly 1.9–2.2", giving a typical 3-month total near 6". Interannual variability is large, driven by ENSO: La Niña winters can total ~3" or less, while El Niño winters with frequent frontal rains can exceed 10–12".

Homestead's Dec–Feb is the dry season, with monthly normals ~1.5–2.2" (total ~5.5"). Winter rainfall is highly variable with ENSO: La Niña winters can total 2–3", while El Niño winters with frequent frontal passages can exceed 10–12". Quantiles reflect this skewed distribution.

Likely a SW Gulf coast city (e.g., Cape Coral/Fort Myers/Sarasota). Dec–Feb is the dry season there, averaging ~2 in/month (~6 in total) from frontal passages. Dry winters (La Niña) can drop totals to ~3 in; wet El Niño winters can push 11+ in. Estimates reflect peninsular Gulf coast dry-season climatology.

North Port (SW Gulf coast) winter is dry season: monthly normals ~1.8-2.5 in (Dec-Feb), totaling ~6 in. Winter rainfall is strongly ENSO-modulated: La Niña winters can drop below 3 in, El Niño winters with frequent frontal rains can exceed 11-12 in. Quantiles reflect this high interannual variability.

Dec–Feb is Florida's dry season. South FL averages ~5–6" total (frontal rain rare), central ~7", north FL ~10–12" (more winter fronts). Unknown city plus interannual variability (ENSO: El Niño winters much wetter, La Niña drier) widens the range. Median ~7"; dry outcome ~3.5"; wet El Niño winter in north FL could reach ~13".

DJF is Florida's dry season. North FL averages ~3-3.5 in/month from frontal systems (~9-10" total); South FL ~1.5-2 in/month (~5-6"). A random FAWN station has median near 7". ENSO drives large interannual swings: La Niña winters can drop below 4", strong El Niño winters can exceed 13-15". Quantiles blend spatial and yearly variability.
